results.title: Distributive Justice and Public Policy
results.fullText: This paper argues that debates over distributive justice in public policy founder on an equivocation between two questions that are routinely run together: what a just distribution would be, and what a legitimate procedure for deciding distributions is. I contend that keeping them apart dissolves several apparent impasses and reframes others as genuine but tractable. Consider the familiar standoff between those who evaluate policies by their patterns of outcome and those who evaluate them by the fairness of the process that generates them. Each side accuses the other of ignoring something essential, and each is right. A pattern theory that is indifferent to how a distribution comes about will endorse outcomes secured by illegitimate means; a procedural theory indifferent to outcomes will bless a fair process whose results are predictably ruinous for the worst off. The lesson is not that one theory must defeat the other but that they answer different questions and a complete view needs both. With the distinction in hand, the policy disputes look different. When we argue about a tax schedule, we are usually not disagreeing about the ideal pattern in the abstract; we are disagreeing about which institutions can be trusted to track reasons under conditions of uncertainty and self-interest. That is a question about legitimate procedure, and it admits of evidence in a way that the abstract pattern question does not. I develop this through three cases: progressive taxation, the design of social insurance, and the allocation of educational resources. In each, I show that the most heated disagreements concern procedural trust rather than the underlying distributive ideal, and that recognizing this opens room for agreement that the framing as a clash of distributive ideals conceals. I anticipate two objections. The first holds that the procedural question simply pushes the distributive question back a step, since we judge procedures by the outcomes they tend to produce. I accept the interdependence but deny that it collapses the distinction: we can and do evaluate procedures by features other than their outcomes, such as whether they give the affected a voice. The second objection holds that my view is too sanguine about the tractability of procedural disputes. Here I concede that tractable is not the same as easy, and that the institutional knowledge required is substantial. But a hard empirical question is a better thing to be left with than an irresoluble clash of ideals, and that, I argue, is the dialectical gain the distinction secures.
